buildWall.name: Build the wall
buildWall.description: Build a complete wall of 55 bricks using nested loops.

buildWall.name: Build a wall of 55 bricks
buildWall.description: Create a complete brick wall pattern using nested loops.

onlyOneRectangleCall.question: I can only call `rectangle` once. How do I draw 55 bricks?
onlyOneRectangleCall.answer: Put the single `rectangle` call inside a loop. Each iteration changes the x and y so a new brick is drawn in a new place.

wholeGrid.question: How do I draw a whole grid rather than just one row?
wholeGrid.answer: Use nested loops. The outer loop steps through the rows (changing y), and the inner loop steps through the bricks in that row (changing x).

offsetRows.question: How do I deal with the rows being offset differently?
offsetRows.answer: Look closely at the picture. Every other row is shifted sideways and has a different number of bricks. So you'll want to handle the two kinds of row differently, choosing a starting position and a brick count for each.

oddEven.question: How do I tell if a row number is odd or even?
oddEven.answer: The `%` (modulo) operator gives you the remainder after dividing. Dividing a row number by 2 gives you a way to tell odd rows apart from even ones, which you can then test inside an `if`.

sizesAndSpacings.question: What sizes and spacings do I use?
sizesAndSpacings.answer: Every brick is `20` wide and `10` tall, so x goes up by `20` between bricks in a row, and y changes by `10` between rows.

rectangle.description: Draw a rectangle at position (left, top) with the given width, height, and color
